query: current use of biomolecular techniques in aerobiological monitoring of pollen

retrieved_docs.title: Dust Mite Allergy -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Other than dust mites, house dust allergy can be caused by the following allergen sources: Mold. The fungus grows in a humid environment like house basements, kitchens, gardens, and bathrooms. The spores of the mold become airborne, which on inhalation leads to allergic rhinitis. Cockroaches. These are found in a variety of environments. The saliva, fecal matter, and shed off parts of cockroaches serve as potential allergens for triggering allergies. Around 68 percent of households in the USA are known to have cockroach allergens. The percentage increases further in urban areas. Pollen. Flowers, grass, weeds, and trees are sources of pollens that constitute an essential component of household dust. These are a common cause for triggering allergic reactions. Pets. Fur, feathers, animal hair, dander, saliva, and fecal matter all serve as allergens. [31] Dust mite allergy can be confused with non-allergic forms of rhinitis. [32] These include the following:

retrieved_docs.title: Dust Mite Allergy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The quantification of dust mite allergens from the house can be done through immunochemical assays, namely, RAST inhibition technique, sandwich radio or enzyme immunoassays, or MAb assays. However, their use is limited due to the requirement of trained laboratory personal and sophisticated equipment.

retrieved_docs.title: Dust Mite Allergy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoroenzyme immunoassays

retrieved_docs.title: Dust Mite Allergy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Basophil activation test (BAT). It is a quantitative test that detects the activation markers on the basophil surface in whole blood. This test can be performed in patients who are already taking antihistamines. It also demonstrates a functional response. However, inconsistent results may be present because of the different varieties of commercial testing kits and varied protocols used. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Allergic and Environmentally Induced Asthma -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Biomarkers of inflammation are being evaluated for their usefulness in the diagnosis of asthma. Biomarkers include total and differential cell count and mediator assays in sputum, blood, urine, and exhaled air.

retrieved_docs.title: Dust Mite Allergy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Nasal provocation test. It identifies and quantifies the clinical relevance of dust mite allergen. The respiratory mucosa is exposed to the dust mite allergen, and the consequent clinical reactions are monitored. Anterior rhinomanometry and acoustic rhinometry are then used for the graphical display of the changes in nasal airflow and patency. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Atopy -- Evaluation -- Allergic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Skin prick tests that utilize various allergens from animals, plants, food, pathogens, and environmental pollutants. A radioallergosorbent test (RAST) to determine specific IgE antibodies.

retrieved_docs.title: Dust Mite Allergy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). It can detect both total and selective IgE levels. Taking cross-reactivity of IgE into consideration, competitive ELISA can be used. The disadvantage is that ELISA cannot be used for a thorough analysis of multiple allergens.

retrieved_docs.title: Allergy Immunotherapy -- Clinical Significance -- Asthma
retrieved_docs.content: Some studies have shown that giving immunotherapy for allergic rhinitis early can prevent the development of asthma in children. [20] [21] [22] A study by Grembiale in 2000 showed that allergy immunotherapy to house dust mite helped to prevent the development of asthma. [22] In the preventative asthma study(PAT) children aged 5 to 12 years were given a subcutaneous injection of grass pollen and/or birch pollen for 3 years and was found to have a decrease in the development of asthma over a 10 year follow up. [20] In the Grass pollen asthma prevention trial (GAP), sublingual treatment with grass pollen also showed reduced asthma symptoms and medication scores. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Wheat Allergy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: For patients being evaluated for baker's asthma, the lower limit with a positive predictive value of 100 percent was 2.32 kU/L for specific wheat IgE and 5.0 mm for wheat on SPT with wheat flour extracts. [1] [33] The gold standard to confirm the diagnosis of wheat-induced occupational allergy is a bronchial challenge test which consists of a nebulized aqueous flour solution in increasing concentrations (0.01, 0.1, 10, and 100 mg/mL) or by inhaling wheat flour dust. [1] Baker's asthma is diagnosed if a bronchial challenge test induces at minimum a 20% decrease in forced expiratory volume in one second (FEV1) or a three-fold increase in bronchial hyperreactivity with an increase in sputum eosinophilia. [1] [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Atopy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The list of occupational allergens causing IgE-mediated allergic asthma includes animal products (obtained from cows, pigs, mice, dogs, cats, and horses), insect dust (from mealworms, storage mites, cockroaches, bees, and flies), plant products (such as fruits, vegetables, seeds, leaves, pollens, flours, plant, grain, and cotton dust, castor beans, tobacco, and weeping fig). Other allergens include gums and extracts (such as western red, California redwood, and exotic woods), microbial agents (fungal allergens, alginates, protozoa, bacteria, and fungi), enzymes (such as papain, hog trypsin, pancreatic extracts, subtilisin, and pineapple bromelain), therapeutic agents (such as penicillins, tetracycline, cephalosporins, sulfonamides, and spiramycin), sterilizing agents (such as chloramides), inorganic chemicals (such as metal fumes and salts, aluminum, cobalt, fluoride, nickel, platinum, vanadium, and zinc) and organic chemicals (such as amines, anhydrides, and azodicarbonamide). [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Atopy -- Treatment / Management -- Allergic Rhinitis
retrieved_docs.content: Environmental measures involve avoiding an allergen based on clinical history. A positive skin test or RAST alone is not sufficient. Environmental control also involves removing household pets, frequent dusting to minimize house dust, and avoiding toys and other objects that collect dust. Using air-cleaning devices to reduce airborne allergens may be helpful. Pollen and outdoor mold growth must be prevented.

retrieved_docs.content: In vitro techniques

retrieved_docs.title: Dust Mite Allergy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Present symptoms, past medical history of allergic manifestations, home environment, and circumstances prompt the clinician to carry out further tests for the diagnosis of dust mite allergy. In vivo techniques

retrieved_docs.title: Pathergy Test -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Results of the pathergy test have been known to vary widely between populations and even between different investigators studying the same population. The positivity rate for pathergy tests in BD is highest in countries along the Silk Route, which includes the Middle East, Far East, and Mediterranean Basin. [6] By contrast, BD patients from the US, UK, and other Northern European nations have significantly lower reported rates for positive pathergy tests. Numerous studies have also demonstrated a decline in the prevalence of positive pathergy tests over time since the 1980s. [12] Genetic, environmental, and procedure-related variations have all been proposed as possible factors that can influence and interfere with pathergy testing. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Dust Mite Allergy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Radio allergen sorbent test (RAST). It is an in vitro test for the detection of IgE levels. The serum IgE of the patient bound to the allergen is immobilized on a solid substrate, and then these are detected using radiolabelled anti-IgE antibodies. Its use has become limited due to the availability of better techniques like ELISA. [21]

This taxonomy clarifies systemic versus inhaled routes, adrenergic subtypes, and the composition of combination bronchodilator products, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate pharmacotherapy for optimal management of obstructive airway diseases and related allergic manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2644
retrieved_docs.content: Allergic reactions represent a broad category of immune‑mediated responses, and chronic allergic rhinitis (الْتِهابُ الأَنْفِ الأَرَجِيّ الدائم) is a specific form of such reactions. Foods (potraviny) are common triggers that can precipitate allergic reactions, and bran, being a type of food, may act as an allergen in susceptible individuals. Recognizing that certain foods can provoke allergic responses, including chronic allergic rhinitis, is important for patient counseling, avoidance strategies, and overall management of allergic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1664
retrieved_docs.content: Aurothioglucose and sodium aurothiomalate are both classified within the structural class of allergens, indicating that each of these gold‑containing compounds has the potential to provoke allergic reactions. Recognizing that both agents belong to the allergen class is important for clinicians when evaluating drug safety, monitoring for hypersensitivity, and managing patients who may be exposed to these substances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1730
retrieved_docs.content: Beclomethasone, fluticasone and Momesalic are all members of the class of corticosteroid nasal preparations for topical use. As intranasal glucocorticoids, they act on the nasal mucosa to suppress inflammatory mediators, reduce edema and relieve symptoms of conditions such as allergic rhinitis, nasal polyposis and other inflammatory nasal disorders. Because they are applied locally rather than taken systemically, these agents provide effective anti‑inflammatory therapy with a lower risk of systemic side‑effects compared with oral corticosteroids, while sharing a common mechanism of glucocorticoid receptor activation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2851
retrieved_docs.content: Asthma, the chronic respiratory disorder marked by airway hyperresponsiveness, is clinically similar to the condition described in Arabic as الرَبْو. The corticosteroid medication triamcinolonacetonid may prevent الرَبْو, indicating that anti‑inflammatory therapy can reduce its occurrence or severity. Environmental exposure to air pollution, expressed in Czech as znečištění ovzduší, is also related to the development or exacerbation of الرَبْو, underscoring the influence of airborne irritants on respiratory health. Recognizing the clinical overlap between asthma and الرَبْو, together with the preventive potential of triamcinolonacetonid and the impact of air pollution, can inform comprehensive management strategies for patients experiencing these interconnected respiratory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1917
retrieved_docs.content: Promethazin‑hydrochlorid is classified within the phenothiazine structural class (fenothiaziny) and may be used therapeutically to treat الْتِهابُ الأَنْفِ الأَرَجِيّ الدائم (chronic allergic rhinitis). Recognizing its phenothiazine‑derived chemistry helps clinicians understand its pharmacologic profile, while its potential efficacy in managing chronic allergic rhinitis supports its inclusion in treatment plans for patients with persistent nasal allergy symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1752
retrieved_docs.content: Ciclesonide and flunisolide are both classified as corticosteroid nasal preparations for topical use, meaning they belong to the group of nasal corticosteroid medications applied locally to the nasal mucosa. As members of this therapeutic class, both agents are used to deliver anti‑inflammatory effects directly to the nasal passages, helping to manage conditions such as allergic rhinitis and other inflammatory nasal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 30

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2310
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroid nasal preparations for topical use constitute a therapeutic class of intranasal medications designed to deliver anti‑inflammatory corticosteroids directly to the nasal mucosa. Both Momesalic and budesonid are members of this class, sharing the common purpose of reducing nasal inflammation in conditions such as allergic rhinitis, chronic sinusitis, and nasal polyposis. By acting locally within the nasal passages, these agents provide targeted symptom relief while minimizing systemic exposure, making them valuable options in the management of upper airway inflammatory disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2725
retrieved_docs.content: Flutikason and ciclesonide are both classified as corticosteroid nasal preparations for topical use. As members of this therapeutic class, they are intranasal glucocorticoids designed to deliver anti‑inflammatory effects directly to the nasal mucosa. These agents are commonly employed to manage conditions such as allergic rhinitis, nasal congestion, and nasal polyps by reducing mucosal edema, suppressing inflammatory mediator release, and improving nasal airflow. Their topical administration minimizes systemic exposure while providing targeted symptom relief, making them important options in the pharmacologic management of upper airway inflammatory disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1812
retrieved_docs.content: Allergic reactions represent a broad category of hypersensitivity responses, and chronic allergic rhinitis (الْتِهابُ الأَنْفِ الأَرَجِيّ الدائم) is a specific, persistent manifestation of these reactions affecting the nasal passages. Diphenhydramine hydrochloride may be employed as a therapeutic option to alleviate the symptoms of chronic allergic rhinitis, highlighting the role of antihistamine medication in managing long‑term allergic nasal inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1416
retrieved_docs.content: Antiallergic inhalants for obstructive airway diseases, excluding corticosteroids, are classified as “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases, inhalants” within the ATC (Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical) system, indicating that they belong to the broader group of inhaled medications used to treat obstructive airway conditions while being distinct from corticosteroid inhalants.
